content.text: Selecting the right data structure is one of the highest-leverage decisions in agentic AI code. A planning agent scheduling parallel tool calls, a retrieval pipeline deduplicating fetched chunks, and a multi-agent router ordering messages by urgency all have fundamentally different access patterns — and each maps cleanly to a different classical structure. This document walks through the six structures most relevant to agent orchestration (stack, queue, priority queue, hash map, graph, and tree), explains their internal mechanics, and shows how to apply them in real Python agent code.

is a disciplined way of organizing and storing values so that a particular set of operations — insert, remove, lookup, iterate — can be performed with known time and space complexity. Unlike an algorithm (a procedure), a data structure is a container that shapes how an algorithm accesses its data.

standard-library modules. Most agentic AI orchestration logic can be expressed with these tools alone; external libraries (networkx, sortedcontainers) become worthwhile only when graph algorithms or sorted-set operations are central to the workload.
The six structures most relevant to agents
— call-stack emulation, DFS plan expansion, undo history, nested tool contexts
— ordered task pipelines, BFS plan expansion, sliding context windows
— urgency-driven scheduling, Dijkstra-style shortest-path in plan graphs
— O(1) tool lookup, visited-node tracking, memoization, deduplication
— task dependency DAGs, multi-agent routing topologies, knowledge graphs
— hierarchical document structure, subtask decomposition, prefix tries for token routing

An agent that calls tools in the wrong order produces incorrect results. An agent that walks a cyclic dependency graph without tracking visited nodes loops forever. An agent that pops from the front of a plain Python list is spending O(n) time per step — invisible at 10 tasks, catastrophic at 10,000.
Structure choice directly determines three properties that matter in production agent systems:
— LIFO, FIFO, or priority order are qualitatively different behaviours, not just performance variants. Confusing them produces wrong answers, not just slow ones.
— the wrong container in a hot loop (e.g. pop(0) on a list) degrades from O(1) to O(n) per operation and turns a millisecond agent step into a seconds-long bottleneck.
documents intent in a way that a bare list cannot. Readable orchestration code is easier to debug when something goes wrong at 3 a.m.
— a deque physically prevents random-access insertion, making FIFO semantics a structural guarantee rather than a convention.
— each structure comes with documented O(·) for every operation, so performance reasoning is first-principles rather than empirical guess-work.
— stacks and queues combine with graphs naturally: DFS uses a stack, BFS uses a queue, Dijkstra uses a heap, all over the same adjacency-list graph.
— Python's built-in structures use C-level arrays and hash tables; a
of 100k items is faster and lighter than the equivalent list.
handle the vast majority of agent orchestration patterns without third-party dependencies.
— every choice is an explicit trade-off. A hash map gives O(1) lookup but O(n) worst-case for iteration in insertion order (though
— an adjacency-list graph is just a dict of lists, but correct BFS/DFS, cycle detection, and topological sort require careful implementation. Bugs here silently produce wrong execution orders.
— a hash map caching every LLM response eliminates redundant API calls but can exhaust memory; unbounded caches need eviction policies (LRU, TTL).
— Python's built-in structures are not thread-safe. Async agents accessing a shared
from multiple coroutines need asyncio queues (
— a list of five tasks never needs a heap or deque; optimizing prematurely adds complexity with no measurable benefit.
A stack maintains a sequence where the most recently inserted element is always removed next. Python's built-in list implements a stack with
(pop), both O(1) amortized because lists are backed by dynamic arrays that double capacity on overflow.

Stacks arise in agent code wherever the current context must be saved and restored: a tool that calls a sub-tool saves its own state on a stack and pops it when control returns, mirroring the CPU call stack. Depth-first search of a plan graph uses an explicit stack to avoid Python's recursion limit.
(double-ended queue) is backed by a doubly-linked list of fixed-size blocks. Both
is O(n) because all remaining elements shift left — this single mistake has caused real production performance regressions in agent frameworks.
Queues model any workflow where fairness or arrival order matters: tool calls should generally execute in the order they were queued, and BFS explores nodes in level order.
module provides a binary min-heap over a plain list. The heap property guarantees that the smallest element is always at index 0.
run in O(log n) time. Because
compares tuples lexicographically, the standard idiom is to push
triples so that equal priorities break ties by a monotone counter rather than by comparing the items themselves (which may not be comparable).

Priority queues power urgency-based scheduling: a critical error notification preempts a routine summarisation task; Dijkstra's algorithm on a dependency graph finds the shortest critical path through a DAG.
Hash map and hash set — O(1) membership

are open-addressing hash tables. Average-case insert, delete, and lookup are O(1); worst-case is O(n) under pathological key collisions, which never occurs with string or integer keys in practice. Hash maps are the workhorses of agent orchestration: tool registries map tool names to handler functions, memoization caches map serialized inputs to outputs, and visited sets track already-explored nodes during graph traversal.

is a set of vertices V and edges E connecting them. An adjacency list represents G as a dict mapping each vertex to its list of neighbors, consuming O(V + E) space — the most practical representation for sparse agent-task graphs. A directed acyclic graph (DAG) encodes task dependencies: edge A → B means 'A must complete before B can start'. Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm using a queue, or DFS-based) produces a valid execution order.

A tree is a connected acyclic graph with a designated root. Trees model hierarchical data: file system paths, JSON document structure, multi-level subtask decomposition, and token-prefix tries for fast routing decisions. In Python, a tree node is typically a dataclass holding a value and a list of children; recursion or an explicit stack handles traversal.
The diagram below shows each of the six structures as they appear within an agentic AI system, with their key operations and typical use-cases annotated.

attrs.alt: Six core data structures used in agentic AI: stack, queue, priority queue, hash map, graph/DAG, and tree

content.text: DFS traversal of a plan graph without hitting Python's recursion limit
Undo / rollback: each state snapshot is pushed; rollback pops the last one
Nested tool contexts: the outer tool's state is saved before the inner call
whenever you need the oldest element first (use a queue)
BFS plan expansion: all nodes at depth k are processed before depth k+1
Tool-call pipeline: calls must execute in arrival order for reproducibility
Sliding context window: oldest messages are dropped from the left as new ones arrive
Urgency scheduling: critical errors preempt background indexing tasks
A* / Dijkstra planning: always expand the lowest-cost frontier node first
Rate-limit retry: retry sooner for high-priority requests
when all tasks have equal priority (overhead is unnecessary)
Memoization / response cache: avoid redundant LLM calls for identical inputs
Visited-node set during graph traversal to prevent infinite loops
ordered iteration by value (use a sorted list or heap)
Task dependency DAG: ensures steps execute only after their prerequisites complete
Multi-agent routing: which agent receives which message type
Knowledge graph: entities and relationships for multi-hop reasoning
when data is strictly sequential or hierarchical with no cross-links
Subtask decomposition: root = goal, children = sub-goals, leaves = atomic actions
JSON / XML document structure: recursive parse and render
Trie (prefix tree): fast longest-prefix match for command routing
when cross-links exist between branches (use a graph instead)
The following complete example implements a generic
that enforces dependency-based execution order using topological sort (Kahn's algorithm).
This pattern combines a hash map for O(1) tool dispatch with a second hash map as a response cache, eliminating redundant LLM calls when the same tool is invoked with identical arguments.
Example 2 — graph-walking agent with cycle detection
Agents that traverse a knowledge graph or plan DAG must track visited nodes. This example performs DFS using an explicit stack (avoiding recursion-depth limits) with a visited set for cycle safety.

from __future__ import annotations
from collections import defaultdict

# Adjacency list: node → [neighbors]
KnowledgeGraph = dict[str, list[str]]

def dfs_collect(graph: KnowledgeGraph, start: str) -> list[str]:
    """
    Depth-first traversal. Returns nodes in visit order.
    Safe on cyclic graphs; never revisits a node.
    """
    visited: set[str] = set()
    stack: list[str] = [start]
    order: list[str] = []

    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()           # LIFO — most recent first
        if node in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(node)
        order.append(node)
        # push neighbors in reverse so left-most is explored first
        for neighbor in reversed(graph.get(node, [])):
            if neighbor not in visited:
                stack.append(neighbor)

    return order

# Build a small knowledge graph (with a back-edge to test cycle safety)
kg: KnowledgeGraph = {
    "agents":      ["tools", "memory", "planning"],
    "tools":       ["search", "code_exec"],
    "memory":      ["vector_db", "agents"],   # back-edge → agents (cycle)
    "planning":    ["task_graph"],
    "search":      [],
    "code_exec":   [],
    "vector_db":   [],
    "task_graph":  [],
}

nodes = dfs_collect(kg, "agents")
print("Visit order:", nodes)
# agents → planning → task_graph → memory → vector_db → tools → code_exec → search
# 'agents' is NOT re-visited despite the back-edge from memory

Consider a document research agent that receives a user question, fans out to multiple search tools, deduplicates fetched URLs, and then synthesizes a response. This pipeline uses four structures simultaneously:
— the work queue of pending URL-fetch tasks (FIFO for fairness)
— a visited-URL set to deduplicate fetches across search results
— a tool registry mapping tool names to async handler coroutines
— a priority queue ranking fetched chunks by relevance score before sending to the LLM

from __future__ import annotations
import asyncio, heapq, itertools
from collections import deque
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Chunk:
    score: float
    text: str
    url: str

_COUNTER = itertools.count()

async def fetch_url(url: str) -> Chunk:
    """Stub: real impl calls httpx / aiohttp."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    return Chunk(score=1.0 / (hash(url) % 10 + 1), text=f"content of {url}", url=url)

async def research_agent(query: str, search_results: list[str]) -> str:
    visited: set[str] = set()
    fetch_queue: deque[str] = deque()
    chunks_heap: list[tuple[float, int, Chunk]] = []  # min-heap by negative score

    # Seed queue
    for url in search_results:
        if url not in visited:
            fetch_queue.append(url)
            visited.add(url)

    # Fetch concurrently (simplified: sequential here)
    while fetch_queue:
        url = fetch_queue.popleft()
        chunk = await fetch_url(url)
        # negate score → min-heap acts as max-heap for relevance
        heapq.heappush(chunks_heap, (-chunk.score, next(_COUNTER), chunk))

    # Take top-3 chunks by relevance
    top_chunks = []
    for _ in range(min(3, len(chunks_heap))):
        _, _, chunk = heapq.heappop(chunks_heap)
        top_chunks.append(chunk.text)

    context = "\n".join(top_chunks)
    return f"Answer based on:\n{context}"

# Run
result = asyncio.run(research_agent(
    "What is RAG?",
    ["https://example.com/rag1", "https://example.com/rag2",
     "https://example.com/rag1"],   # duplicate — filtered by visited set
))
print(result)
Popping from the front of a list shifts all remaining elements left, an O(n) operation. In a loop over 10,000 tasks, this turns O(n) total work into O(n²).

from collections import deque

tasks_list = list(range(10_000))
tasks_deque = deque(range(10_000))

# BAD — O(n) per pop
while tasks_list:
    item = tasks_list.pop(0)   # shifts 9999, then 9998, ...

# GOOD — O(1) per pop
while tasks_deque:
    item = tasks_deque.popleft()
Mistake 2 — graph traversal without a visited set
A graph with cycles causes infinite recursion or an infinite loop if visited nodes are not tracked.
Mistake 3 — raw dict comparison as heap elements
Dictionaries are not comparable in Python 3; pushing
when two items have equal priority and Python tries to compare the dicts as a tiebreaker.

import heapq, itertools

_COUNTER = itertools.count()

# BAD — crashes with TypeError when priorities tie
heap = []
heapq.heappush(heap, (1, {"name": "task_a"}))
heapq.heappush(heap, (1, {"name": "task_b"}))   # TypeError: '<' not supported

# GOOD — tiebreaker counter avoids ever comparing the payload
heap = []
heapq.heappush(heap, (1, next(_COUNTER), {"name": "task_a"}))
heapq.heappush(heap, (1, next(_COUNTER), {"name": "task_b"}))

# BAD — all callers share the same visited set
def dfs(node, graph, visited=[]):   # list is created ONCE
    ...

# GOOD
def dfs(node, graph, visited=None):
    if visited is None:
        visited = set()
    ...
for any queue (FIFO) workload; reserve lists for stack (LIFO) usage or random access.
triples in heaps to break ties deterministically without requiring payloads to be comparable.
catches category errors before they reach runtime and documents the contract.
Keep graph edge direction consistent: choose either 'dependency → dependent' or 'dependent → dependency' and document it; mixing directions causes subtle traversal bugs.
with a size limit) to prevent memory growth in long-running agents.
for adjacency lists to avoid explicit key-existence checks when adding edges.
Validate DAGs on construction: run topological sort once at startup and fail fast on cycles rather than discovering them during execution.
The table below summarises the time complexity of the core operations for each structure:
Amortized O(1) append; pop(0) is O(n)
Only min element is O(1) via peek
Average case; worst case O(n) on collision
Python has no built-in BST; use sortedcontainers

For agent-scale workloads (hundreds to low thousands of tasks), all Python built-in structures are fast enough. Bottlenecks almost always occur in I/O (LLM API latency, vector database queries) rather than in the orchestration data structures themselves. Profile before optimizing.

At true scale — millions of tasks, distributed multi-agent systems — in-process structures must give way to external systems: Redis lists/sorted-sets replace deque/heapq, graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) replace in-memory adjacency lists, and distributed caches (Redis, Memcached) replace local dicts.
— O(1) double-ended queue; the default choice for task queues
— dict that auto-initialises missing keys; ideal for adjacency lists
— hash map counting occurrences; useful for token-frequency analysis
— insertion-ordered dict; building block for LRU caches
— binary min-heap over lists; standard priority scheduling
— decorator-based memoization with configurable LRU eviction
— O(log n) insertion into a maintained sorted list; no C extension required, pure Python but fast
— full-featured graph library: BFS, DFS, shortest path, DAG utilities, cycle detection — worth adding when graph algorithms are the primary workload
— access Redis lists (LPUSH/RPOP = queue), sorted sets (ZADD/ZPOPMIN = priority queue), and hashes (HSET/HGET = hash map) for distributed agent deployments
Using asyncio queues in async agents

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass(order=True)
class PriorityItem:
    priority: int
    payload: Any = field(compare=False)

async def producer(q: asyncio.PriorityQueue) -> None:
    await q.put(PriorityItem(priority=5, payload="bg_task"))
    await q.put(PriorityItem(priority=1, payload="urgent"))
    await q.put(PriorityItem(priority=3, payload="normal"))
    await q.put(None)   # sentinel

async def consumer(q: asyncio.PriorityQueue) -> None:
    while True:
        item = await q.get()
        if item is None:
            break
        print(f"Processing [{item.priority}]: {item.payload}")
        q.task_done()

async def main() -> None:
    q: asyncio.PriorityQueue = asyncio.PriorityQueue()
    await asyncio.gather(producer(q), consumer(q))

asyncio.run(main())
# Processing [1]: urgent
# Processing [3]: normal
# Processing [5]: bg_task
When choosing between structures, the primary question is: what operation is in the hot path?
O(log n) push vs O(n) insort for large n
O(log n) insert + O(1) sorted iteration

In-memory structures have no built-in size limits. An agent that indefinitely enqueues tasks without consuming them, or that caches every LLM response without eviction, will eventually exhaust available memory and crash. Always set explicit bounds:

from collections import deque

# Bounded sliding-window context (evicts oldest on overflow)
context_window: deque[str] = deque(maxlen=20)
context_window.append("new message")  # auto-evicts oldest if len > 20

# LRU cache with size limit
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=1_000)
def expensive_embed(text: str) -> tuple[float, ...]:
    ...  # call embedding API
to loop forever or exhaust the stack. Always validate a graph at construction time, not at execution time:

If user-controlled strings flow directly into a dict or set, an adversary could craft keys that collide under Python's hash function, degrading performance to O(n) per operation. Python 3.3+ mitigates this with randomised hash seeds (

) by default, but relying on user-supplied keys as cache keys without validation is still a bad practice. Always sanitize or normalize keys (e.g. lowercase, strip whitespace) before using them as dict keys in a cache.
Python's built-in structures are not thread-safe. Use
in async agents. In multi-threaded agents (rare in Python due to the GIL, but possible with
from the standard library, which include internal locking.
Think of each structure as matching a real-world dispatch metaphor:
= a stack of plates. You always take from the top (last in, first out).
= a checkout line. The first person in is the first served.
= an emergency room triage. The most critical patient is seen first, regardless of arrival order.
= a library card catalogue. Given a title (key), you find the shelf location (value) in one step.
= a road map. Any city (node) connects to others via roads (edges); you can plan routes and find cycles.
= a company org chart. One CEO at the root; every employee has exactly one manager; no cross-links.

When designing agent orchestration, ask: 'When I need the next item, how do I decide which one that is?' Arrival order → queue. Most recent → stack. Highest priority → heap. By name → dict. Depends on others → graph. Part of a hierarchy → tree.
Choose structure based on access pattern: FIFO → deque, LIFO → list, priority → heapq, key lookup → dict, membership → set, dependencies → graph, hierarchy → tree.
as a queue — it is O(n). Use
when priorities tie and payloads are not comparable.
Always track visited nodes with a set when traversing graphs; cyclic graphs cause infinite loops without it.
Validate DAGs for cycles at construction time using topological sort or DFS colouring; fail fast rather than looping indefinitely at runtime.
so coroutines can safely await on empty queues.
) to prevent unbounded memory growth in long-running agents.
For distributed or high-scale deployments, replace in-process structures with Redis (lists, sorted sets, hashes) or dedicated graph databases rather than scaling in-memory structures.
